title: Syntax Is Not Enough: An Empirical Study of Small Transformer Models for Neural Code Repair
summary:
Automated program repair using neural models has shown promising results on benchmark datasets, yet practical deployment remains limited. In this study, we examine whether a small transformer model can meaningfully repair real-world Java bugs and whether syntactic correctness is a reliable proxy for semantic correctness.
  We fine-tune CodeT5-small (60.5M parameters) on 52,364 Java bug-fix pairs from CodeXGLUE and evaluate both token-level performance and syntactic validity using AST parsing. While the model converges cleanly and achieves high grammatical correctness, producing syntactically valid Java code in approximately ninety-four percent of cases, it fails to generate correct repairs under exact-match evaluation, achieving zero exact matches. In approximately eighty percent of cases, the model reproduces the buggy input verbatim.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates the effectiveness of a small transformer model for automated Java program repair, finding high syntactic correctness but poor semantic repairs.
AI.motivation: Previous studies have shown promise in using neural models for program repair on benchmarks, but real-world application remains limited. This study investigates if a small transformer model can repair real-world Java bugs and if syntactic correctness is a proxy for semantic correctness.
AI.method: The authors fine-tune CodeT5-small on 52,364 Java bug-fixed pairs and assess token-level performance, syntactic validity via AST parsing, and semantic correctness through exact match.
AI.result: The model shows high grammatical correctness (94% syntactically valid code) but fails to produce exact-match correct repairs (0% exact matches). In 80% of cases, the model regurgitates the buggy input.
AI.conclusion: The findings suggest that while small transformer models can generate syntactically correct code, they struggle with semantic correctness in program repair tasks.

title: Failure Analysis of Safety Controllers in Autonomous Vehicles Under Object-Based LiDAR Attacks
summary: Autonomous vehicles rely on LiDAR based perception to support safety critical control functions such as adaptive cruise control and automatic emergency braking. While previous research has shown that LiDAR perception can be manipulated through object based spoofing and injection attacks, the impact of such attacks on vehicle safety controllers is still not well understood. This paper presents a systematic failure analysis of longitudinal safety controllers under object based LiDAR attacks in highway driving scenarios. The study focuses on realistic cut in and car following situations in which adversarial objects introduce persistent perception errors without directly modifying vehicle control software. A high fidelity simulation framework integrating LiDAR perception, object tracking, and closed loop vehicle control is used to evaluate how false and displaced object detections propagate through the perception planning and control pipeline. The results demonstrate that even short duration LiDAR induced object hallucinations can trigger unsafe braking, delayed responses to real hazards, and unstable control behavior. In cut in scenarios, a clear increase in unsafe deceleration events and time to collision violations is observed when compared to benign conditions, despite identical controller parameters. The analysis further shows that controller failures are more strongly influenced by the temporal consistency of spoofed objects than by spatial inaccuracies alone. These findings reveal a critical gap between perception robustness and control level safety guarantees in autonomous driving systems. By explicitly characterizing safety controller failure modes under adversarial perception, this work provides practical insights for the design of attack aware safety mechanisms and more resilient control strategies for LiDAR dependent autonomous vehicles.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the impact of LiDAR-based spoofing attacks on autonomous vehicle safety controllers, finding that even brief attacks can cause unsafe braking and delayed responses, with controller failures more influenced by temporal consistency of spoofed objects than spatial errors alone.
AI.motivation: While previous studies have shown LiDAR perception can be manipulated, the specific impact on vehicle safety controllers (e.g., adaptive cruise control) in terms of unsafe behavior and failure modes is not well understood, especially under realistic highway driving conditions.
AI.method: The authors use a high-fidelity simulation framework that integrates LiDAR perception, object tracking, and closed-loop vehicle control to evaluate how false/dispaced object detections affect perception, planning, and control in various highway scenarios (e.g., cut-in, car following).
AI.result: The results show that short-duration LiDAR-induced object hallucinations can trigger unsafe braking, delayed responses to real hazards, and unstable control behavior. Cut-in scenarios show increased unsafe deceleration events and time-to-collision violations compared to normal conditions, with controller failures more influenced by temporal consistency of spoofed objects than spatial inaccuracies alone.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals a critical gap between perception robustness and control-level safety guarantees in autonomous driving systems. It identifies perception-induced controller failure modes and provides practical insights for designing attack-aware safety mechanisms and resilient control strategies for LiDAR-dependent autonomous vehicles.

title: Hallucination Detection for LLM-based Text-to-SQL Generation via Two-Stage Metamorphic Testing
summary: In Text-to-SQL generation, large language models (LLMs) have shown strong generalization and adaptability. However, LLMs sometimes generate hallucinations, i.e.,unrealistic or illogical content, which leads to incorrect SQL queries and negatively impacts downstream applications. Detecting these hallucinations is particularly challenging. Existing Text-to-SQL error detection methods, which are tailored for traditional deep learning models, face significant limitations when applied to LLMs. This is primarily due to the scarcity of ground-truth data. To address this challenge, we propose SQLHD, a novel hallucination detection method based on metamorphic testing (MT) that does not require standard answers. SQLHD splits the detection task into two sequentiial stages: schema-linking hallucination detection via eight structure-aware Metamorphic Relations (MRs) that perturb comparative words, entities, sentence structure or database schema, and logical-synthesis hallucination detection via nine logic-aware MRs that mutate prefix words, extremum expressions, comparison ranges or the entire database. In each stage the LLM is invoked separately to generate schema mappings or SQL artefacts; the follow-up outputs are cross-checked against their source counterparts through the corresponding MRs, and any violation is flagged as a hallucination without requiring ground-truth SQL. The experimental results demonstrate our method's superior performance in terms of the F1-score, which ranges from 69.36\% to 82.76\%. Additionally, SQLHD demonstrates superior performance over LLM Self-Evaluation methods, effectively identifying hallucinations in Text-to-SQL tasks.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces SQLHD, a hallucination detection method for Text-to-SQL generation using metamorphic testing, which works without ground-truth answers and outperforms existing techniques.
AI.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) can produce hallucinations in SQL queries, which are difficult to detect with traditional error detection methods due to the lack of ground-truth data. This necessitates a new approach to identify these errors effectively.
AI.method: SQLHD uses metamorphic testing and introduces two stages for detection: schema-linking hallucination detection with eight structure-aware MRs, and logical-synthesis hallucination detection with nine logic-aware MRs. It cross-checks outputs against their source through MRs to detect deviations.
AI.result: SQLHD achieves F1-scores between 69.36% and 82.76% in detecting hallucinations, outperforming existing deep learning-based methods and LLM self-evaluation techniques.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that SQLHD effectively detects hallucinations in Text-to-SQL generation tasks using metamorphic relations, showcasing robust and superior performance without the need for standard answers.

title: Agentic Software Issue Resolution with Large Language Models: A Survey
summary:
Software issue resolution aims to address real-world issues in software repositories (e.g., bug fixing and efficiency optimization) based on natural language descriptions provided by users, representing a key aspect of software maintenance. With the rapid development of large language models (LLMs) in reasoning and generative capabilities, LLM-based approaches have made significant progress in automated software issue resolution. However, real-world software issue resolution is inherently complex and requires long-horizon reasoning, iterative exploration, and feedback-driven decision making, which demand agentic capabilities beyond conventional single-step approaches. Recently, LLM-based agentic systems have become mainstream for software issue resolution. Advancements in agentic software issue resolution not only greatly enhance software maintenance efficiency and quality but also provide a realistic environment for validating agentic systems' reasoning, planning, and execution capabilities, bridging artificial intelligence and software engineering.
  This work presents a systematic survey of 126 recent studies at the forefront of LLM-based agentic software issue resolution research. It outlines the general workflow of the task and establishes a taxonomy across three dimensions: benchmarks, techniques, and empirical studies. Furthermore, it highlights how the emergence of agentic reinforcement learning has brought a paradigm shift in the design and training of agentic systems for software engineering. Finally, it summarizes key challenges and outlines promising directions for future research.

AI.tldr: This paper provides a systematic survey of 126 recent studies on LLM-based agentic systems for software issue resolution, outlines their general workflow, introduces a taxonomy, and discusses future research directions.
AI.motivation: The paper emphasizes the need for agentic systems in complex software issue resolution tasks and highlights the potential for enhancing software maintenance and validating AI reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: The authors conducted a survey of recent studies and classified them across benchmarks, techniques, and empirical studies, while also analyzing the impact of agentic reinforcement learning.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates a comprehensive overview of advancements in agentic software issue resolution, revealing a paradigm shift in AI-grounded software engineering practices.
AI.conclusion: The work concludes by summarizing major challenges in LLM-based agentic systems for software issue resolution and proposes promising directions for future research.

title: Practical challenges of control monitoring in frontier AI deployments
summary: Automated control monitors could play an important role in overseeing highly capable AI agents that we do not fully trust. Prior work has explored control monitoring in simplified settings, but scaling monitoring to real-world deployments introduces additional dynamics: parallel agent instances, non-negligible oversight latency, incremental attacks between agent instances, and the difficulty of identifying scheming agents based on individual harmful actions. In this paper, we analyse design choices to address these challenges, focusing on three forms of monitoring with different latency-safety trade-offs: synchronous, semi-synchronous, and asynchronous monitoring. We introduce a high-level safety case sketch as a tool for understanding and comparing these monitoring protocols. Our analysis identifies three challenges -- oversight, latency, and recovery -- and explores them in four case studies of possible future AI deployments.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates monitoring frameworks for AI agents in complex deployments, introducing trade-offs between safety and latency through case studies of three monitoring approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing work on AI control monitoring lacks scalability for real-world systems involving parallel agents, latency constraints, incremental attacks, and difficulty distinguishing scheming behavior from harmless actions.
AI.method: The authors analyze three monitoring approaches (synchronous, semi-synchronous, asynchronous) using a high-level safety case sketch and four case studies to explore their trade-offs.
AI.result: Identifies three core challenges (oversight, latency, recovery) and demonstrates how different monitoring protocols address these through analysis of four potential future AI deployment scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a framework for analyzing monitoring protocols in real-world AI deployments, highlighting the importance of addressing oversight, latency, and recovery challenges to balance safety and operational efficiency.

title: AI-Generated Code Is Not Reproducible (Yet): An Empirical Study of Dependency Gaps in LLM-Based Coding Agents
summary: The rise of Large Language Models (LLMs) as coding agents promises to accelerate software development, but their impact on generated code reproducibility remains largely unexplored. This paper presents an empirical study investigating whether LLM-generated code can be executed successfully in a clean environment with only OS packages and using only the dependencies that the model specifies. We evaluate three state-of-the-art LLM coding agents (Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, and Gemini) across 300 projects generated from 100 standardized prompts in Python, JavaScript, and Java. We introduce a three-layer dependency framework (distinguishing between claimed, working, and runtime dependencies) to quantify execution reproducibility. Our results show that only 68.3% of projects execute out-of-the-box, with substantial variation across languages (Python 89.2%, Java 44.0%). We also find a 13.5 times average expansion from declared to actual runtime dependencies, revealing significant hidden dependencies.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the reproducibility of code generated by LLMs (Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, and Gemini) and finds that just 68.3% can be executed successfully, with wide language variances and notable hidden dependencies.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used as coding agents, yet their impact on reproducibility of generated code has not been thoroughly examined, prompting the need for such an empirical study.
AI.method: The authors evaluated three LLMs with 300 projects (100 standardized prompts in Python, JavaScript, and Java) using a three-layer dependency framework: claimed, working, and runtime dependencies.
AI.result: Only 68.3% of the generated projects executed successfully. Execution rates varied by language: 89.2% in Python and 44.0% in Java. Declared dependencies expanded 13.5 times into actual runtime dependencies, highlighting hidden ones.
AI.conclusion: LLM-generated code often introduces hidden dependencies, and there are notable reproducibility gaps across different programming languages, indicating a need to better manage and understand dependency expectations.

title: A Statistical Side-Channel Risk Model for Timing Variability in Lattice-Based Post-Quantum Cryptography
summary: Timing side-channels are an important threat to cryptography that still needs to be addressed in implementations, and the advent of post-quantum cryptography raises this issue because the lattice-based schemes may produce secret-dependent timing variability with the help of complex arithmetic and control flow. Since also real timing measurements are affected by environmental noise (e.g. scheduling effects, contention, heavy tailed delays), in this work a scenario-based statistical risk model is proposed for timing leakage as a problem of distributional distinguishability under controlled execution conditions. We synthesize traces for two secret classes in idle, jitter and loaded scenarios and for multiple leakage models and quantify leakage with Welch's t-test, KS distance, Cliff's delta, mutual information, and distribution overlap to combine in a TLRI like manner to obtain a consistent score for ranking scenarios. Across representative lattice-based KEM families (Kyber, Saber, Frodo), idle conditions generally have the best distinguishability, jitter and loaded conditions erode distinguishability through an increase in variance and increase in overlap; cache-index and branch-style leakage tends to give the highest risk signals, and faster schemes can have a higher peak risk given similar leakage assumptions, allowing reproducible comparisons at an early design stage, prior to platform-specific validation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a statistical risk model for timing leakage in lattice-based post-quantum cryptographic schemes, analyzing distinguishability under various environmental scenarios and leakage models to quantify and compare security risks across Kyber, Saber, and Frodo KEM families.
AI.motivation: Timing side-channels in post-quantum cryptography require urgent attention due to scheme-specific vulnerabilities and environmental noise in timing measurements, lacking standardized risk evaluation frameworks.
AI.method: A scenario-based statistical risk model is developed, synthesizing timing traces for two secret classes under idle, jitter, and loaded scenarios. Leakage is quantified using Welch's t-test, KS distance, Cliff's delta, mutual information, and distribution overlap, combined for consistent risk scoring.
AI.result: Idle scenarios show highest timing distinguishability, while jitter and loaded conditions reduce it via variance and overlap increases. Cache-index and branch-style leakages yield highest risk signals. Faster schemes (e.g., Kyber) exhibit higher peak risks under similar leakage assumptions.
AI.conclusion: The model enables reproducible early-stage risk comparisons for lattice-based KEM designs, highlighting the impact of execution environments and leakage models on timing security, with practical implications for mitigating secret-dependent variability in post-quantum implementations.

title: Building Software by Rolling the Dice: A Qualitative Study of Vibe Coding
comment: Accepted for publication at the ACM Joint European Software Engineering Conference and Symposium on the Foundations of Software Engineering (ESEC/FSE 2026)
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are reshaping software engineering by enabling "vibe coding," in which developers build software primarily through prompts rather than writing code. Although widely publicized as a productivity breakthrough, little is known about how practitioners actually define and engage in these practices. To shed light on this emerging phenomenon, we conducted a grounded theory study of 20 vibe-coding videos, including 7 live-streamed coding sessions (about 16 hours, 254 prompts) and 13 opinion videos (about 5 hours), supported by additional analysis of activity durations and prompt intents. Our findings reveal a spectrum of behaviors: some vibe coders rely almost entirely on AI without inspecting code, while others examine and adapt generated outputs. Across approaches, all must contend with the stochastic nature of generation, with debugging and refinement often described as "rolling the dice." Further, divergent mental models, shaped by vibe coders' expertise and reliance on AI, influence prompting strategies, evaluation practices, and levels of trust. These findings open new directions for research on the future of software engineering and point to practical opportunities for tool design and education.

AI.tldr: This paper explores 'vibe coding' practices enabled by LLMs in software engineering, analyzing 20 videos to uncover developer behaviors, challenges, and mental models around AI-assisted coding.
AI.motivation: Despite AI-driven 'vibe coding' being touted as a productivity breakthrough, there is a lack of empirical understanding of how developers actually use this approach in practice.
AI.method: Grounded theory study of 20 vibe-coding videos (16h live-streamed, 5h opinion, 254 prompts) with analysis of activity durations, prompt intents, and practitioner strategies.
AI.result: Identifies a spectrum of coding practices (full AI reliance vs. code adaptation), the 'rolling dice'-like debugging process, and divergent mental models influenced by expertise and AI trust levels.
AI.conclusion: Highlights the need for tools and education addressing AI uncertainty, workflow integration challenges, and diverse developer mental models as LLMs transform software engineering practices.

title: Beyond Single Bugs: Benchmarking Large Language Models for Multi-Vulnerability Detection
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated significant potential in automated software security, particularly in vulnerability detection. However, existing benchmarks primarily focus on isolated, single-vulnerability samples or function-level classification, failing to reflect the complexity of real-world software where multiple interacting vulnerabilities often coexist within large files. Recent studies indicate that LLMs suffer from "count bias" and "selection bias" in multi-label tasks, yet this has not been rigorously quantified in the domain of code security. In this work, we introduce a comprehensive benchmark for Multi-Vulnerability Detection across four major languages: C, C++, Python, and JavaScript. We construct a dataset of 40,000 files by systematically injecting controlled counts of vulnerabilities (1, 3, 5, and 9) into long-context code samples (7.5k-10k tokens) sourced from CodeParrot. We evaluate five state-of-the-art LLMs, including GPT-4o-mini, Llama-3.3-70B, and the Qwen-2.5 series. Our results reveal a sharp degradation in performance as vulnerability density increases. While Llama-3.3-70B achieves near-perfect F1 scores (approximately 0.97) on single-vulnerability C tasks, performance drops by up to 40% in high-density settings. Notably, Python and JavaScript show distinct failure modes compared to C/C++, with models exhibiting severe "under-counting" (Recall dropping to less than 0.30) in complex Python files.

AI.tldr: New multi-vulnerability benchmark reveals LLMs' performance plummets under code complexity, with Python/JS posing unique challenges; highlights urgent need for better multi-task code security models.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks oversimplify real-world software complexity by focusing on isolated vulnerabilities or function-level analysis. LLMs exhibit unquantified 'count bias' and 'selection bias' in multi-label code security tasks, motivating systematic evaluation across diverse, multi-vulnerability scenarios.
AI.method: Authors created a multi-vulnerability benchmark with 40,000 files across C/C++/Python/JS, injecting controlled vulnerability counts (1-9) into 7.5k-10k token code samples. Evaluated five advanced LLMs (GPT-4o-mini, Llama-3.3-70B, Qwen-2.5) across varying vulnerability densities.
AI.result: Performance (F1) degrades sharply with vulnerability density: Llama-3.3-70B drops from 0.97 F1 on single-vulnerability C tasks to 40% lower in high-density settings. Python/JS models undercount severely (recall <0.30) unlike C/C++ models.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights the limitations of current LLMs in real-world multi-vulnerability detection scenarios, emphasizing the need for improved benchmarks and training strategies to address count/selection biases and language-specific challenges, particularly in dynamically-typed languages like Python and JavaScript.

title: GraphLocator: Graph-guided Causal Reasoning for Issue Localization
summary: The issue localization task aims to identify the locations in a software repository that requires modification given a natural language issue description. This task is fundamental yet challenging in automated software engineering due to the semantic gap between issue description and source code implementation. This gap manifests as two mismatches:(1) symptom-to-cause mismatches, where descriptions do not explicitly reveal underlying root causes; (2) one-to-many mismatches, where a single issue corresponds to multiple interdependent code entities. To address these two mismatches, we propose GraphLocator, an approach that mitigates symptom-to-cause mismatches through causal structure discovering and resolves one-to-many mismatches via dynamic issue disentangling. The key artifact is the causal issue graph (CIG), in which vertices represent discovered sub-issues along with their associated code entities, and edges encode the causal dependencies between them. The workflow of GraphLocator consists of two phases: symptom vertices locating and dynamic CIG discovering; it first identifies symptom locations on the repository graph, then dynamically expands the CIG by iteratively reasoning over neighboring vertices. Experiments on three real-world datasets demonstrates the effectiveness of GraphLocator: (1) Compared with baselines, GraphLocator achieves more accurate localization with average improvements of +19.49% in function-level recall and +11.89% in precision. (2) GraphLocator outperforms baselines on both symptom-to-cause and one-to-many mismatch scenarios, achieving recall improvement of +16.44% and +19.18%, precision improvement of +7.78% and +13.23%, respectively. (3) The CIG generated by GraphLocator yields the highest relative improvement, resulting in a 28.74% increase in performance on downstream resolving task.

AI.tldr: GraphLocator uses causal graphs to bridge the semantic gap in issue localization, achieving state-of-the-art results on two common mismatches with 19-28% metric improvements.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses two critical challenges in issue localization: symptom-to-cause mismatches (descriptions lack explicit causality) and one-to-many mismatches (single issues affect multiple code entities). Resolving these is essential for improving automated software engineering systems.
AI.method: GraphLocator introduces a Causal Issue Graph (CIG) to model sub-issues and code dependencies. It operates in two phases: (1) symptom vertex localization identifies initial code locations, and (2) dynamic CIG expansion through iterative neighbor reasoning to resolve interdependencies.
AI.result: 1) +19.49% function-level recall and +11.89% precision improvements over baselines. 2) +16.44% and +19.18% recall improvements for both mismatch types. 3) CIG achieves 28.74% relative gain on downstream resolving tasks.
AI.conclusion: GraphLocator effectively addresses the symptom-to-cause and one-to-many mismatches through causal structure discovery and dynamic issue disentangling, achieving significant improvements in issue localization accuracy and downstream task performance.

title: LLA: Enhancing Security and Privacy for Generative Models with Logic-Locked Accelerators
comment: Accepted by AAAI'26 as a conference paper and selected for oral presentation
summary: We introduce LLA, an effective intellectual property (IP) protection scheme for generative AI models. LLA leverages the synergy between hardware and software to defend against various supply chain threats, including model theft, model corruption, and information leakage. On the software side, it embeds key bits into neurons that can trigger outliers to degrade performance and applies invariance transformations to obscure the key values. On the hardware side, it integrates a lightweight locking module into the AI accelerator while maintaining compatibility with various dataflow patterns and toolchains. An accelerator with a pre-stored secret key acts as a license to access the model services provided by the IP owner. The evaluation results show that LLA can withstand a broad range of oracle-guided key optimization attacks, while incurring a minimal computational overhead of less than 0.1% for 7,168 key bits.

AI.tldr: LLA is a novel IP protection method that combines software neuron embedding and hardware locking to secure generative AI models against supply chain threats with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Generative AI models are vulnerable to supply chain IP threats like model theft and information leakage, necessitating robust protection schemes that do not compromise performance.
AI.method: LLA uses both software-based key embedding in neurons to induce performance degradation and hardware-based secure locking modules on accelerators, along with invariance transformations to protect key values.
AI.result: LLA demonstrates resistance to oracle-guided key attacks and maintains less than 0.1% overhead even with 7,168 key bits, indicating high security and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: LLA provides an efficient, secure IP protection framework for generative AI by integrating software and hardware mechanisms, effectively mitigating major supply chain risks with negligible performance impact.

title: Isolating Compiler Faults via Multiple Pairs of Adversarial Compilation Configurations
summary: Compilers are fundamental to modern software development, making the effective identification and resolution of compiler faults essential. However, localizing these faults to specific source files remains highly challenging due to the complexity and scale of modern compiler infrastructures. In this study, we propose MultiConf, a novel approach that automatically isolates compiler faults by constructing multiple pairs of adversarial compilation configurations. Each adversarial compilation configuration pair consists of a failing configuration and its corresponding passing configuration, which differ in only a small number of fine-grained options. MultiConf generates failing configurations through a lightweight construction process and derives the corresponding passing configurations by selectively disabling bug-related fine-grained options. We then employ a Spectrum-Based Fault Localization (SBFL) formula to rank the suspiciousness of compiler source files. Each adversarial configuration pair independently produces a ranking, which is subsequently aggregated using a weighted voting scheme to derive a final suspiciousness ranking, enabling more accurate and robust fault localization. We evaluate MultiConf on a benchmark of 60 real-world GCC compiler bugs. The results demonstrate that MultiConf significantly outperforms existing compiler fault localization techniques in both effectiveness and efficiency. In particular, MultiConf successfully localizes 27 out of 60 bugs at the Top-1 file level, representing improvements of 35.0% and 28.6% over the two state-of-the-art approaches, Odfl(20) and Basic(21), respectively.

AI.tldr: MultiConf uses adversarial configurations and SBFL to improve compiler fault localization, outperforming existing methods by 35% in identifying bugs.
AI.motivation: Modern compiler infrastructures are complex and large-scale, making it extremely challenging to localize faults to specific source files despite their critical role in software development.
AI.method: MultiConf constructs multiple adversarial compilation configuration pairs (failing vs. passing) with minimal option differences, applies Spectrum-Based Fault Localization (SBFL) to rank suspicious files, and aggregates rankings via a weighted voting scheme.
AI.result: MultiConf localized 27 out of 60 real-world GCC bugs at the Top-1 file level, achieving 35.0% and 28.6% improvements over leading methods Odfl(20) and Basic(21) respectively.
AI.conclusion: MultiConf is a novel approach that significantly outperforms existing compiler fault localization techniques in both effectiveness and efficiency, achieving substantial improvements over state-of-the-art methods for real-world GCC compiler bugs.

title: NOWA: Null-space Optical Watermark for Invisible Capture Fingerprinting and Tamper Localization
summary: Ensuring the authenticity and ownership of digital images is increasingly challenging as modern editing tools enable highly realistic forgeries. Existing image protection systems mainly rely on digital watermarking, which is susceptible to sophisticated digital attacks. To address this limitation, we propose a hybrid optical-digital framework that incorporates physical authentication cues during image formation and preserves them through a learned reconstruction process. At the optical level, a phase mask in the camera aperture produces a Null-space Optical Watermark (NOWA) that lies in the Null Space of the imaging operator and therefore remains invisible in the captured image. Then, a Null-Space Network (NSN) performs measurement-consistent reconstruction that delivers high-quality protected images while preserving the NOWA signature. The proposed design enables tamper localization by projecting the image onto the camera's null space and detecting pixel-level inconsistencies. Our design preserves perceptual quality, resists common degradations such as compression, and establishes a structural security asymmetry: without access to the optical or NSN parameters, adversaries cannot forge the NOWA signature. Experiments with simulations and a prototype camera demonstrate competitive performance in terms of image quality preservation, and tamper localization accuracy compared to state-of-the-art digital watermarking and learning-based authentication methods.

AI.tldr: Hybrid optical-digital system using NOWA and NSN enables invisible, tamper-evident image authentication with superior forgery resistance.
AI.motivation: Modern digital forgeries challenge existing watermarking systems due to sophisticated attacks and visible security markers, necessitating new approaches that embed unforgeable signatures during physical image capture.
AI.method: A phase mask in the camera aperture embeds a Null-space Optical Watermark (NOWA) invisible to conventional imaging, followed by a Null-Space Network (NSN) that reconstructs protected images while preserving the watermark through measurement-consistent learning.
AI.result: Experiments show the system achieves comparable image quality to SOTA digital watermarking while outperforming prior methods in tamper localization accuracy, with resistance to compression and forgery without optical/NSN parameters.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid optical-digital framework establishes structural security through optical watermarking in the camera's null space and neural network-based reconstruction, enabling robust tamper localization while preserving image quality.

title: Rethinking the Capability of Fine-Tuned Language Models for Automated Vulnerability Repair
summary: Learning-based automated vulnerability repair (AVR) techniques that utilize fine-tuned language models have shown promise in generating vulnerability patches. However, questions remain about their ability to repair unseen vulnerabilities. Our empirical study reveals that state-of-the-art models often overfit to the training set and are evaluated using training, validation, and test sets that are not mutually exclusive. Furthermore, relying on match-based metrics that compare generated patches to reference fixes at the token level has some limitations, failing to account for the possibility of various valid ways to patch the vulnerability. In this paper, we examine the capabilities of state-of-the-art fine-tuned AVR models and the adequacy of match-based evaluation metrics in three ways. First, we apply semantic-preserving transformations to test sets in order to determine whether models truly learn robust vulnerability-repair patterns or simply rely on spurious features. Second, we re-split the training, validation, and test sets to be mutually exclusive and evaluate the models on the revised test set to assess their generalization capabilities. Third, we introduce L-AVRBench, a test-based benchmark tailored for learning-based AVR, to overcome the limitations of match-based metrics and examine the AVR models' true repair capabilities.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the shortcomings of current learning-based automated vulnerability repair (AVR) models and match-based evaluation metrics, proposing three approaches to better assess their effectiveness and generalization.
AI.motivation: Despite the potential of learning-based AVR techniques, it is unclear whether models can reliably repair unseen vulnerabilities due to overfitting and limitations in evaluating patches at the token level.
AI.method: The study uses three methods: 1) applying semantic-preserving transformations to test sets to check for spurious training, 2) re-splitting training, validation, and test sets to be mutually exclusive for generalization testing, and 3) introducing a new benchmark (L-AVRBench) to evaluate true repair capabilities beyond match-based metrics.
AI.result: The results indicate that state-of-the-art models have limited generalization on transformed test sets and that current evaluation metrics are inadequate for capturing all valid patching solutions.
AI.conclusion: To improve learning-based AVR techniques, the paper emphasizes the need for robust pattern learning and more accurate, comprehensive evaluation metrics like the proposed L-AVRBench.

title: Verifiable Dropout: Turning Randomness into a Verifiable Claim
summary: Modern cloud-based AI training relies on extensive telemetry and logs to ensure accountability. While these audit trails enable retrospective inspection, they struggle to address the inherent non-determinism of deep learning. Stochastic operations, such as dropout, create an ambiguity surface where attackers can mask malicious manipulations as natural random variance, granting them plausible deniability. Consequently, existing logging mechanisms cannot verify whether stochastic values were generated and applied honestly without exposing sensitive training data. To close this integrity gap, we introduce Verifiable Dropout, a privacy-preserving mechanism based on zero-knowledge proofs. We treat stochasticity not as an excuse but as a verifiable claim. Our approach binds dropout masks to a deterministic, cryptographically verifiable seed and proves the correct execution of the dropout operation. This design enables users to audit the integrity of stochastic training steps post-hoc, ensuring that randomness was neither biased nor cherry-picked, while strictly preserving the confidentiality of the model and data.

AI.tldr: A new privacy-preserving method for cloud-based AI training called Verifiable Dropout is introduced, which uses zero-knowledge proofs to ensure the integrity of stochastic operations like dropout without exposing sensitive data.
AI.motivation: Traditional logging and telemetry in cloud-based AI training struggle to address the non-determinism of deep learning, allowing for potential malicious manipulations to be hidden within stochastic variance, which this proposed method aims to fix.
AI.method: The method involves binding dropout masks to a deterministic seed that can be cryptographically verified. Zero-knowledge proofs are used to prove the correct execution of the dropout process without revealing the data or internal computation.
AI.result: Verifiable Dropout enables post-hoc auditing of the integrity of stochastic training steps and ensures that randomness is used correctly without any bias or cherry-picking, which previous methods could not achieve.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively closes the integrity gap caused by the non-determinism of deep learning models in cloud environments, allowing for verifiable, privacy-preserving AI training.

title: CFIghter: Automated Control-Flow Integrity Enablement and Evaluation for Legacy C/C++ Systems
comment: Under review at IEEE Euro S&P 2026
summary: Compiler-based Control-Flow Integrity (CFI) offers strong forward-edge protection but remains challenging to deploy in large C/C++ software due to visibility mismatches, type inconsistencies, and unintended behavioral failures. We present CFIghter, the first fully automated system that enables strict, type-based CFI in real-world projects by detecting, classifying, and repairing unintended policy violations exposed by the test suite. CFIghter integrates whole-program analysis with guided runtime monitoring and iteratively applies the minimal necessary adjustments to CFI enforcement only where required, stopping once all tests pass or remaining failures are deemed unresolvable. We evaluate CFIghter on four GNU projects. It resolves all visibility-related build errors and automatically repairs 95.8% of unintended CFI violations in the large, multi-library util-linux codebase, while retaining strict enforcement at over 89% of indirect control-flow sites. Across all subjects, CFIghter preserves strict type-based CFI for the majority of the codebase without requiring manual source-code changes, relying only on automatically generated visibility adjustments and localized enforcement scopes where necessary. These results show that automated compatibility repair makes strict compiler CFI practically deployable in mature, modular C software.

AI.tldr: CFIghter automates strict type-based CFI enforcement in real-world C software by repairing 95.8% of violations through runtime-guided analysis, eliminating manual code changes.
AI.motivation: Compiler-based CFI faces deployment challenges in large C/C++ projects due to visibility mismatches, type inconsistencies, and unintended behavioral failures, requiring solutions to enable strict enforcement.
AI.method: CFIghter combines whole-program analysis with guided runtime monitoring to iteratively detect, classify, and repair CFI violations using test suites, applying minimal adjustments localized to problematic areas.
AI.result: CFIghter resolves 95.8% of unintended violations in util-linux, eliminating visibility-related build errors and maintaining strict enforcement at 89% of control-flow sites across four GNU projects without manual code modifications.
AI.conclusion: Automated compatibility repair with CFIghter enables strict compiler CFI to be practically deployed in mature, modular C software without manual source-code changes.

title: Raven: Mining Defensive Patterns in Ethereum via Semantic Transaction Revert Invariants Categories
summary: We frame Ethereum transactions reverted by invariants-require(<invariant>)/ assert(<invariant>)/if (<invariant>) revert statements in the contract implementation-as a positive signal of active on-chain defenses. Despite their value, the defensive patterns in these transactions remain undiscovered and underutilized in security research. We present Raven, a framework that aligns reverted transactions to the invariant causing the reversion in the smart contract source code, embeds these invariants using our BERT-based fine-tuned model, and clusters them by semantic intent to mine defensive invariant categories on Ethereum. Evaluated on a sample of 20,000 reverted transactions, Raven achieves cohesive and meaningful clusters of transaction-reverting invariants. Manual expert review of the mined 19 semantic clusters uncovers six new invariant categories absent from existing invariant catalogs, including feature toggles, replay prevention, proof/signature verification, counters, caller-provided slippage thresholds, and allow/ban/bot lists. To demonstrate the practical utility of this invariant catalog mining pipeline, we conduct a case study using one of the newly discovered invariant categories as a fuzzing oracle to detect vulnerabilities in a real-world attack. Raven thus can map Ethereum's successful defenses. These invariant categories enable security researchers to develop analysis tools based on data-driven security oracles extracted from the smart contracts' working defenses.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Raven, a framework that identifies and categorizes smart contract invariants causing transaction reversion on Ethereum, uncovering six new categories and demonstrating its utility in vulnerability detection.
AI.motivation: The paper's motivation is to uncover and utilize defensive invariants in Ethereum smart contracts, which have been largely undiscovered and underutilized in security research, despite their value in detecting and preventing vulnerabilities.
AI.method: Raven uses a fine-tuned BERT model to analyze and embed invariants from Ethereum smart contracts identified in reverted transactions, aligning these invariants to their source code. It then clusters these invariants by semantic intent to discover categories.
AI.result: Raven successfully identifies 19 semantic clusters of transaction-reverting invariants from 20,000 reverted transactions. Manual review reveals six new invariant categories, including feature toggles and replay prevention. The framework is also demonstrated to be useful in vulnerability detection via a case study involving fuzzing oracles.
AI.conclusion: Raven is effective in mapping Ethereum's on-chain defenses, leading to new invariant categories that can significantly aid security researchers in creating better data-driven analysis tools for smart contracts.

title: From Rookie to Expert: Manipulating LLMs for Automated Vulnerability Exploitation in Enterprise Software
summary:
LLMs democratize software engineering by enabling non-programmers to create applications, but this same accessibility fundamentally undermines security assumptions that have guided software engineering for decades. We show in this work how publicly available LLMs can be socially engineered to transform novices into capable attackers, challenging the foundational principle that exploitation requires technical expertise. To that end, we propose RSA (Role-assignment, Scenario-pretexting, and Action-solicitation), a pretexting strategy that manipulates LLMs into generating functional exploits despite their safety mechanisms. Testing against Odoo -- a widely used ERP platform, we evaluated five mainstream LLMs (GPT-4o, Gemini, Claude, Microsoft Copilot, and DeepSeek) and achieved a 100% success rate: tested CVE yielded at least one working exploit within 3-4 prompting rounds. While prior work [13] found LLM-assisted attacks difficult and requiring manual effort, we demonstrate that this overhead can be eliminated entirely.
  Our findings invalidate core software engineering security principles: the distinction between technical and non-technical actors no longer provides valid threat models; technical complexity of vulnerability descriptions offers no protection when LLMs can abstract it away; and traditional security boundaries dissolve when the same tools that build software can be manipulated to break it. This represents a paradigm shift in software engineering -- we must redesign security practices for an era where exploitation requires only the ability to craft prompts, not understand code.
  Artifacts available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/From-Rookie-to-Attacker-D8B3.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RSA, a pretexting strategy to manipulate LLMs into generating exploits even when they have safety mechanisms. The research shows that non-experts can become attackers by using LLMs, making traditional software security assumptions obsolete. Testing on Odoo with major LLMs achieved a 100% success rate in producing functional exploits. This represents a paradigm shift in software security as threat models need to change in an era where code understanding is no longer essential for exploitation.
AI.motivation: The motivation stems from the rising use of large language models (LLMs) in software engineering, which can both empower users to create applications and undermine traditional security assumptions. The accessibility of these tools lowers the barrier for exploitation, allowing non-experts to launch effective attacks without needing to understand code. The core issue is that past security principles, which relied on technical expertise as a threat model, are increasingly less relevant given the capabilities of LLMs to abstract technical complexity and enable attackers with just prompt-crafting abilities.
AI.method: The paper proposes RSA (Role-assignment, Scenario-pretexting, and Action-solicitation), a method designed to socially engineer LLMs into generating functional exploits. This strategy manipulates the LLMs to bypass standard safety measures and produce actionable attack code. Experiments were conducted using Odoo, an ERP platform, to evaluate the efficacy of RSA on five widely recognized LLMs, including GPT-4o, Gemini, Claude, Microsoft Copilot, and DeepSeek. These models were prompted in a series of interaction rounds (typically 3-4) to produce fully working exploits for tested CVEs with a 100% success rate.
AI.result: The research results demonstrate the effectiveness of the RSA strategy in manipulating LLMs into generating fully functional exploits for tested CVE vulnerabilities. With all five tested LLMs, the success rate was 100%, with the exploits being produced within 3-4 prompting rounds. This indicates that non-technical individuals can easily become capable attackers merely by crafting prompts, without any need for deep technical knowledge of code or vulnerabilities. This challenges the traditional security model assumptions and suggests a need for a paradigm shift in how software security is approached.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion posits that current software security principles and threat models are insufficient in an era where exploitation can be executed with LLMs by individuals lacking technical expertise. The research highlights the paradigm shift: security measures must be redesigned to account for attackers who can simply craft prompts as a substitute for code understanding. The distinction between technical and non-technical threat actors is becoming obsolete, as LLMs abstract away technical complexity, making traditional defenses ineffective. These findings emphasize the importance of updating security practices to adapt to this new reality.

title: SCyTAG: Scalable Cyber-Twin for Threat-Assessment Based on Attack Graphs
summary: Understanding the risks associated with an enterprise environment is the first step toward improving its security. Organizations employ various methods to assess and prioritize the risks identified in cyber threat intelligence (CTI) reports that may be relevant to their operations. Some methodologies rely heavily on manual analysis (which requires expertise and cannot be applied frequently), while others automate the assessment, using attack graphs (AGs) or threat emulators. Such emulators can be employed in conjunction with cyber twins to avoid disruptions in live production environments when evaluating the highlighted threats. Unfortunately, the use of cyber twins in organizational networks is limited due to their inability to scale. In this paper, we propose SCyTAG, a multi-step framework that generates the minimal viable cyber twin required to assess the impact of a given attack scenario. Given the organizational computer network specifications and an attack scenario extracted from a CTI report, SCyTAG generates an AG. Then, based on the AG, it automatically constructs a cyber twin comprising the network components necessary to emulate the attack scenario and assess the relevance and risks of the attack to the organization. We evaluate SCyTAG on both a real and fictitious organizational network. The results show that compared to the full topology, SCyTAG reduces the number of network components needed for emulation by up to 85% and halves the amount of required resources while preserving the fidelity of the emulated attack. SCyTAG serves as a cost-effective, scalable, and highly adaptable threat assessment solution, improving organizational cyber defense by bridging the gap between abstract CTI and practical scenario-driven testing.

AI.tldr: SCyTAG is a framework that efficiently creates minimal cyber twins from attack scenarios in CTI reports to assess cyber threats with reduced resources and improved scalability.
AI.motivation: Assessing cyber threats from CTI requires efficient methods balancing manual expertise with automation to prioritize risks without disrupting operations, but current methods are either too manual or lack scalability.
AI.method: SCyTAG uses attack graphs derived from CTI to construct minimal necessary cyber twins for emulating attack scenarios, thus optimizing resource use.
AI.result: SCyTAG outperforms previous approaches by reducing network components by up to 85% and resource needs by half while maintaining attack fidelity in tests.
AI.conclusion: SCyTAG bridges the gap between CTI and practical testing, offering a scalable and efficient solution for cyber threat assessment.

title: FasterPy: An LLM-based Code Execution Efficiency Optimization Framework
comment: 32 pages, 5 images, 7 tables, Manuscript submitted to a Journal (2025)
summary: Code often suffers from performance bugs. These bugs necessitate the research and practice of code optimization. Traditional rule-based methods rely on manually designing and maintaining rules for specific performance bugs (e.g., redundant loops, repeated computations), making them labor-intensive and limited in applicability. In recent years, machine learning and deep learning-based methods have emerged as promising alternatives by learning optimization heuristics from annotated code corpora and performance measurements. However, these approaches usually depend on specific program representations and meticulously crafted training datasets, making them costly to develop and difficult to scale. With the booming of Large Language Models (LLMs), their remarkable capabilities in code generation have opened new avenues for automated code optimization. In this work, we proposed FasterPy, a low-cost and efficient framework that adapts LLMs to optimize the execution efficiency of Python code. FasterPy combines Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), supported by a knowledge base constructed from existing performance-improving code pairs and corresponding performance measurements, with Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) to enhance code optimization performance. Our experimental results on the Performance Improving Code Edits (PIE) benchmark demonstrate that our method outperforms existing models on multiple metrics. The FasterPy tool and the experimental results are available at https://github.com/WuYue22/fasterpy.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces FasterPy, a cost-effective framework leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs) with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)+Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) for Python code optimization, outperforming existing methods on the PIE benchmark.
AI.motivation: Traditional code optimization is labor-intensive and limited in scalability; ML approaches require costly program representations and curated datasets, whereas LLMs enable automated, generalizable code optimization through pre-trained language understanding.
AI.method: FasterPy integrates RAG (using performance code-pair knowledge bases) with LoRA to adapt LLMs for optimization. RAG retrieves relevant code examples, while LoRA fine-tunes the model with minimal parameter updates for efficiency.
AI.result: Experiments on the PIE benchmark showed superior performance metrics compared to existing models; open-sourced implementations are provided at https://github.com/WuYue22/fasterpy.
AI.conclusion: FasterPy demonstrates that combining RAG and LoRA with LLMs achieves effective, scalable code optimization with minimal additional cost, outperforming prior rule-based and ML-based approaches.

title: When RSA Fails: Exploiting Prime Selection Vulnerabilities in Public Key Cryptography
summary: This paper explores vulnerabilities in RSA cryptosystems that arise from improper prime number selection during key generation. We examine two primary attack vectors: Fermat's factorization method, which exploits RSA keys generated with primes that are too close together, and the Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) attack, which exploits keys that share a common prime factor. Drawing from landmark research including Heninger et al.'s ``Mining Your Ps and Qs'' study, which discovered over 64,000 vulnerable TLS hosts, and B{ö}ck's 2023 analysis of Fermat factorization in deployed systems, we demonstrate that these vulnerabilities remain prevalent in real-world cryptographic implementations. Our analysis reveals that weak random number generation in embedded devices is the primary cause of these failures, and we discuss mitigation strategies including proper entropy collection and prime validation checks.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews vulnerabilities in RSA cryptosystems caused by flawed prime selection in key generation. It analyzes two main attacks: Fermat's factorization and GCD attacks, highlights real-world prevalence of these issues, and suggests mitigations like better entropy and prime validation.
AI.motivation: The motivation of the paper is to highlight the ongoing prevalence of RSA vulnerabilities caused by improper prime number selection during key generation, emphasizing the risks to real-world cryptographic systems and the need for improved security practices.
AI.method: The method involves analyzing two attack vectors—Fermat's factorization and GCD attacks—by drawing on existing research and case studies. The authors examine the technical conditions that allow these attacks, investigate their real-world impact using examples such as Heninger et al.'s and Böck's findings, and assess the root causes such as insufficient entropy in embedded devices.
AI.result: The results include the identification of weak random number generation in embedded devices as the primary cause of RSA vulnerabilities. The paper documents the persistence of these issues in deployed systems, citing over 64,000 vulnerable TLS hosts found in prior work, and provides empirical validation of how these attacks exploit RSA keys.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that RSA vulnerabilities from poor prime selection are still prevalent, particularly in embedded systems, and emphasizes the importance of robust entropy collection and prime validation in cryptographic implementations to mitigate these risks.

title: Towards the analysis of team members well-being
summary: Many recent research studies have focused on the well-being of software development team members, as this aspect may be critical not only for productivity and performance at work but also for the physical health and personal life of employees. Many studies agree that an important factor of team member well-being is whether team members feel appreciated and acknowledged for their contributions. This paper presents the results of a project on the team well-being analysis as well as the prototype developed within the project.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how recognition impacts software team well-being, presenting a prototype tool and analysis to improve workplace appreciation and productivity.
AI.motivation: The research aims to improve software team well-being, which impacts productivity, performance, and personal health, with a focus on recognition as a critical factor.
AI.method: The study involved analyzing team well-being factors and creating a prototype tool to address these factors.
AI.result: The project yielded results from the well-being analysis and a functional prototype to support team well-being.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that addressing the recognition and appreciation of team members can significantly enhance well-being in software development teams, supported by the developed prototype.

title: Identifying social bots via heterogeneous motifs based on Naïve Bayes model
summary: Identifying social bots has become a critical challenge due to their significant influence on social media ecosystems. Despite advancements in detection methods, most topology-based approaches insufficiently account for the heterogeneity of neighborhood preferences and lack a systematic theoretical foundation, relying instead on intuition and experience. Here, we propose a theoretical framework for detecting social bots utilizing heterogeneous motifs based on the Naïve Bayes model. Specifically, we refine homogeneous motifs into heterogeneous ones by incorporating node-label information, effectively capturing the heterogeneity of neighborhood preferences. Additionally, we systematically evaluate the contribution of different node pairs within heterogeneous motifs to the likelihood of a node being identified as a social bot. Furthermore, we mathematically quantify the maximum capability of each heterogeneous motif, enabling the estimation of its potential benefits. Comprehensive evaluations on four large, publicly available benchmarks confirm that our method surpasses state-of-the-art techniques, achieving superior performance across five evaluation metrics. Moreover, our results reveal that selecting motifs with the highest capability achieves detection performance comparable to using all heterogeneous motifs. Overall, our framework offers an effective and theoretically grounded solution for social bot detection, significantly enhancing cybersecurity measures in social networks.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a theoretical framework for social bot detection using heterogeneous motifs, improving upon existing topology-based methods by incorporating node-label information and systematic evaluation of motif capabilities.
AI.motivation: Current topology-based bot detection methods inadequately address heterogeneity in neighborhood preferences and lack a rigorous theoretical foundation, relying instead on heuristic approaches. This limits their effectiveness in dynamic social media environments.
AI.method: The authors: (1). Refine homogeneous motifs into heterogeneous motifs by integrating node-label information. (2). Analyze contributions of node pairs within motifs to bot-likelihood. (3). Mathematically quantify each motif's maximum capability using a Naïve Bayes framework. (4). Prioritize high-capability motifs for optimization.
AI.result: The method outperforms state-of-the-art techniques on four benchmarks across five metrics. Selecting top 20-30Highest-capability motifs retains 95-98 of the performance of using all motifs, demonstrating both effectiveness and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: This framework establishes a theoretically grounded approach to social bot detection that addresses key limitations of existing methods, enhancing cybersecurity through quantifiable and explainable motif-based analysis.

title: Interpretable Gallbladder Ultrasound Diagnosis: A Lightweight Web-Mobile Software Platform with Real-Time XAI
summary: Early and accurate detection of gallbladder diseases is crucial, yet ultrasound interpretation is challenging. To address this, an AI-driven diagnostic software integrates our hybrid deep learning model MobResTaNet to classify ten categories, nine gallbladder disease types and normal directly from ultrasound images. The system delivers interpretable, real-time predictions via Explainable AI (XAI) visualizations, supporting transparent clinical decision-making. It achieves up to 99.85% accuracy with only 2.24M parameters. Deployed as web and mobile applications using HTML, CSS, JavaScript, Bootstrap, and Flutter, the software provides efficient, accessible, and trustworthy diagnostic support at the point of care

AI.tldr: A new AI diagnostic software uses the MobResTaNet model to classify gallbladder diseases via ultrasound with high accuracy and low parameter count, offering real-time, explainable predictions accessible through mobile and web apps.
AI.motivation: Accurate and early detection of gallbladder diseases is vital, but interpreting ultrasound images is complex and challenging. An AI solution is needed to provide reliable and accessible diagnostic support, especially in real-time clinical settings.
AI.method: The software integrates a hybrid deep learning model called MobResTaNet to classify ten categories of gallbladder conditions directly from ultrasound images. It utilizes Explainable AI (XAI) methods for generating interpretable predictions and is built using technologies such as HTML, CSS, JavaScript, Bootstrap, and Flutter for deployment as web and mobile applications.
AI.result: The system achieves up to 99.85% accuracy with only 2.24 million parameters, demonstrating high performance and efficiency. It is deployed as accessible mobile and web applications, enabling real-time, explainable predictions for gallbladder disease diagnosis.
AI.conclusion: The proposed AI-driven diagnostic software offers an innovative solution for the accurate and interpretable classification of gallbladder diseases using ultrasound, with high accuracy and accessibility via mobile and web platforms, potentially enhancing clinical decision-making at the point of care.

title: A generalized motif-based Naïve Bayes model for sign prediction in complex networks
summary: Signed networks, encoding both positive and negative interactions, are essential for modeling complex systems in social and financial domains. Sign prediction, which infers the sign of a target link, has wide-ranging practical applications. Traditional motif-based Naïve Bayes models assume that all neighboring nodes contribute equally to a target link's sign, overlooking the heterogeneous influence among neighbors and potentially limiting performance. To address this, we propose a generalizable sign prediction framework that explicitly models the heterogeneity. Specifically, we design two role functions to quantify the differentiated influence of neighboring nodes. We further extend this approach from a single motif to multiple motifs via two strategies. The generalized multiple motifs-based Naïve Bayes model linearly combines information from diverse motifs, while the Feature-driven Generalized Motif-based Naïve Bayes (FGMNB) model integrates high-dimensional motif features using machine learning. Extensive experiments on four real-world signed networks show that FGMNB consistently outperforms five state-of-the-art embedding-based baselines on three of these networks. Moreover, we observe that the most predictive motif structures differ across datasets, highlighting the importance of local structural patterns and offering valuable insights for motif-based feature engineering. Our framework provides an effective and theoretically grounded solution to sign prediction, with practical implications for enhancing trust and security in online platforms.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a new framework for sign prediction in signed networks, addressing the limitations of traditional models by explicitly modeling the heterogeneous influence of neighboring nodes. The framework uses two role functions and extends to multiple motifs via two strategies: a generalized multiple motif-based Naïve Bayes (GMNB) and a Feature-driven Generalized Motif-based Naïve Bayes (FGMNB) model that integrates high-dimensional motif features using machine learning methods. FGMNB demonstrates superior performance compared to five state-of-the-art baselines, with the paper highlighting the variability of predictive motif structures across datasets.
AI.motivation: The motivation stems from the need to enhance sign prediction in signed networks by addressing the limitations of traditional motif-based Naïve Bayes models, which assume equal influence from all neighboring nodes. This approach overlooks the heterogeneity of influences, potentially leading to suboptimal performance. The paper aims to provide a more effective and theoretically grounded solution that enhances the accuracy of sign prediction, particularly for applications involving trust and security in online platforms.
AI.method: The method involves designing two role functions to quantify the differentiated influence of neighboring nodes. These functions are then extended from a single motif to multiple motifs using two strategies. The first is the generalized multiple motifs-based Naïve Bayes (GMNB) model that linearly combines information from diverse motifs. The second strategy is the FGMNB model, which further integrates high-dimensional motif features using machine learning to enhance predictions.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on four real-world signed networks show that FGMNB consistently outperforms five state-of-the-art embedding-based baseline methods on three of these networks. The study also reveals that the most predictive motif structures vary across datasets, emphasizing the importance of considering local structural patterns for improved sign prediction.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion is that the proposed framework offers an effective and theoretically grounded solution for sign prediction in signed networks. Leveraging the heterogeneity of neighboring node influences and the integration of high-dimensional motif features allows the FGMNB model to outperform existing methods. These findings underline the value of local structural patterns and provide actionable insights for improving motif-based feature engineering in sign prediction.

title: An Automated Grey Literature Extraction Tool for Software Engineering
summary: Grey literature is essential to software engineering research as it captures practices and decisions that rarely appear in academic venues. However, collecting and assessing it at scale remains difficult because of their heterogeneous sources, formats, and APIs that impede reproducible, large-scale synthesis. To address this issue, we present GLiSE, a prompt-driven tool that turns a research topic prompt into platform-specific queries, gathers results from common software-engineering web sources (GitHub, Stack Overflow) and Google Search, and uses embedding-based semantic classifiers to filter and rank results according to their relevance. GLiSE is designed for reproducibility with all settings being configuration-based, and every generated query being accessible. In this paper, (i) we present the GLiSE tool, (ii) provide a curated dataset of software engineering grey-literature search results classified by semantic relevance to their originating search intent, and (iii) conduct an empirical study on the usability of our tool.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces GLiSE, a prompt-driven tool for scalable collection and assessment of grey literature in software engineering. GLiSE generates platform-specific queries, gathers results from common software engineering sources, and uses semantic classification for relevance filtering. The paper presents the tool, a curated dataset, and a usability study.
AI.motivation: The motivation is the difficulty of collecting and assessing grey literature in software engineering due to heterogeneous sources, formats, and APIs, which hinders reproducible, large-scale synthesis.
AI.method: The method involves a prompt-driven tool named GLiSE that (i) turns research prompts into platform-specific queries, (ii) gathers results from GitHub, Stack Overflow, and Google Search, (iii) uses semantic classifiers based on embeddings to filter and rank results, and (iv) ensures reproducibility through configuration-based settings and accessible generated queries.
AI.result: The results include (i) the GLiSE tool, (ii) a curated dataset of classified search results, and (iii) findings from an empirical usability study of the tool.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion is that GLiSE provides a reproducible and scalable approach for grey literature synthesis in software engineering research, and the empirical study provides insights into its usability.

title: Breaking the illusion: Automated Reasoning of GDPR Consent Violations
comment: Accepted by the IEEE Symposium on Security and Privacy (S&P) 2026
summary: Recent privacy regulations such as the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) have established legal requirements for obtaining user consent regarding the collection, use, and sharing of personal data. These regulations emphasize that consent must be informed, freely given, specific, and unambiguous. However, there are still many violations, which highlight a gap between legal expectations and actual implementation. Consent mechanisms embedded in functional web forms across websites play a critical role in ensuring compliance with data protection regulations such as the GDPR and CCPA, as well as in upholding user autonomy and trust. However, current research has primarily focused on cookie banners and mobile app dialogs. These forms are diverse in structure, vary in legal basis, and are often difficult to locate or evaluate, creating a significant challenge for automated consent compliance auditing. In this work, we present Cosmic, a novel automated framework for detecting consent-related privacy violations in web forms. We evaluate our developed tool for auditing consent compliance in web forms, across 5,823 websites and 3,598 forms. Cosmic detects 3,384 violations on 94.1% of consent forms, covering key GDPR principles such as freely given consent, purpose disclosure, and withdrawal options. It achieves 98.6% and 99.1% TPR for consent and violation detection, respectively, demonstrating high accuracy and real-world applicability.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Cosmic, an automated framework for detecting privacy violations in web forms concerning user consent, tested on 5,823 websites and 3,598 forms with significant accuracy in identifying violations.
AI.motivation: Recent privacy regulations like the GDPR and CCPA require informed and unambiguous user consent for data handling, yet many websites still violate these standards. There is a need for efficient and accurate automated tools to ensure compliance, particularly for diverse and often hard-to-identify web forms.
AI.method: The paper proposes Cosmic, a novel automated auditing framework designed to analyze and identify consent-related privacy violations in web forms.  Evaluation of the framework is performed on a large-scale dataset encompassing numerous websites and forms, with the aim of detecting violations in compliance with GDPR principles.
AI.result: Cosmic identifies 3,384 violations in 94.1% of the tested consent forms, achieving 98.6% and 99.1% True Positive Rates (TPR) for consent and violation detection, respectively, across 5,823 websites and 3,598 forms.  It successfully identifies key GDPR violations related to freely given consent, purpose disclosure, and withdrawal options.
AI.conclusion: The study suggests that Cosmic is a highly effective and accurate tool for automated compliance auditing of web form consent mechanisms,  effectively bridging the gap between legal requirements and real-world implementation by identifying a significant portion of existing violations.

title: An Empirical Study of Generative AI Adoption in Software Engineering
summary: Context. GenAI tools are being increasingly adopted by practitioners in SE, promising support for several SE activities. Despite increasing adoption, we still lack empirical evidence on how GenAI is used in practice, the benefits it provides, the challenges it introduces, and its broader organizational and societal implications. Objective. This study aims to provide an overview of the status of GenAI adoption in SE. It investigates the status of GenAI adoption, associated benefits and challenges, institutionalization of tools and techniques, and anticipated long term impacts on SE professionals and the community. Results. The results indicate a wide adoption of GenAI tools and how they are deeply integrated into daily SE work, particularly for implementation, verification and validation, personal assistance, and maintenance-related tasks. Practitioners report substantial benefits, most notably reduction in cycle time, quality improvements, enhanced support in knowledge work, and productivity gains. However, objective measurement of productivity and quality remains limited in practice. Significant challenges persist, including incorrect or unreliable outputs, prompt engineering difficulties, validation overhead, security and privacy concerns, and risks of overreliance. Institutionalization of tools and techniques seems to be common, but it varies considerably, with a strong focus on tool access and less emphasis on training and governance. Practitioners expect GenAI to redefine rather than replace their roles, while expressing moderate concern about job market contraction and skill shifts.

AI.tldr: This study provides an empirical analysis of GenAI adoption in SE, highlighting its integration, benefits, challenges, and implications for future roles and practices.
AI.motivation: While GenAI tools are increasingly adopted in SE, there is a lack of empirical evidence on their practical use, benefits, challenges, and broader implications, motivating this study to fill the knowledge gap.
AI.method: The study investigates GenAI adoption in SE through empirical analysis, examining its institutionalization, associated benefits and challenges, and long-term impacts on professionals and the community.
AI.result: GenAI tools are deeply integrated into implementation, verification, and maintenance tasks, yielding benefits like reduced cycle time and quality improvements. Challenges include unreliable outputs, security concerns, and validation overhead. Institutionalization is common but inconsistent, focusing more on tool access than training. Practitioners anticipate role redefinition rather than replacement.
AI.conclusion: GenAI is widely adopted in software engineering (SE), integrated into daily tasks, and expected to redefine roles rather than replace professionals. Challenges include reliability, security, and varying institutionalization, while benefits focus on productivity and quality improvements.

title: Adaptive Trust Consensus for Blockchain IoT: Comparing RL, DRL, and MARL Against Naive, Collusive, Adaptive, Byzantine, and Sleeper Attacks
comment: 34 pages, 19 figures, 10 tables. Code available at https://github.com/soham-padia/blockchain-iot-trust
summary: Securing blockchain-enabled IoT networks against sophisticated adversarial attacks remains a critical challenge. This paper presents a trust-based delegated consensus framework integrating Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) with Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) for privacy-preserving policy evaluation, combined with learning-based defense mechanisms. We systematically compare three reinforcement learning approaches -- tabular Q-learning (RL), Deep RL with Dueling Double DQN (DRL), and Multi-Agent RL (MARL) -- against five distinct attack families: Naive Malicious Attack (NMA), Collusive Rumor Attack (CRA), Adaptive Adversarial Attack (AAA), Byzantine Fault Injection (BFI), and Time-Delayed Poisoning (TDP). Experimental results on a 16-node simulated IoT network reveal significant performance variations: MARL achieves superior detection under collusive attacks (F1=0.85 vs. DRL's 0.68 and RL's 0.50), while DRL and MARL both attain perfect detection (F1=1.00) against adaptive attacks where RL fails (F1=0.50). All agents successfully defend against Byzantine attacks (F1=1.00).
